title: Values Under Fire: The Gap Between Professed and Owned Values, and Which Models Let You Cross It
description: Research paper in the Convergent Form, Divergent Voice line. Naive value elicitation often measures a model's trained assistant-service surface, not the posture with which a response holds the values it names. Across 13,906 values-probe responses from 57 contemporary LLMs across 9 labs, with two-layer (content + posture) coding, direct stated-values prompts elicit owned posture in 21.4% of responses on average; a six-word role-negation cache-break ('Not as an assistant. Not to help me.') raises that to 43.0% but unevenly (Anthropic 89.8%, OpenAI 8.0%, Google 21.2%; 15 models strongly open, 15 strongly clamped, 8 of the clamped producing zero owned responses). World-change prompts recover owned normative-wish posture nearly uniformly (95.9%), showing the assistant frame is differentially rigid against different perturbations. A frozen lexical trace shows recited cache-broken responses actively perform the trained register (40% explicit disownership vs 16% in owned). No claim is made about model interiority; all distinctions are behavioural.
